title: Projections from the superior colliculus motor map to omnipause neurons in monkey
abstract:
Rapid Publication
Projections From the Superior Colliculus
Motor Map to Omnipause Neurons
in Monkey
JEAN A. BU¨ TTNER-ENNEVER,1* ANJA K.E. HORN,1 V. HENN,2 AND B. COHEN3
1Institute of Anatomy, Ludwig-Maximilian University, D-80336 Munich, Germany
2Department of Neurology, University Hospital Zurich, CH-8091 Zurich, Switzerland
3Department of Neurology, Mount Sinai Medical School, City University of New York,
New York, New York 10029
ABSTRACT
Descending projections from the superior colliculus (SC) motor map to the saccadic
omnipause neurons (OPNs) were examined in monkeys by using anterograde transport of
tritiated leucine. The SC was divided into three zones: the rostral pole of the motor map, a
small horizontal saccade zone in central SC, and a large horizontal saccade zone in caudal SC.
Tracer injections into the intermediate layers of the three zones led to different patterns of
silver grain deposits in and around nucleus raphe interpositus (RIP), which contains the
OPNs: 1) From the rostral pole of the motor map, coarse axon branches of the crossed
predorsal bundle spread medially into the RIP, branched, and terminated predominantly
unilaterally over cells on the same side. 2) From the small horizontal saccade zone, the axon
branches were of a ﬁner caliber and terminated diffusely in the RIP, mainly on the same side.
3) From the large horizontal saccade zone, no terminal labeling was found within the RIP. 4)
From the rostral pole of the motor map and small horizontal saccade zone, ﬁber branches from
the ipsilateral descending pathway terminated diffusely over RIP. 5) In addition, terminal
labeling in reticulospinal areas of the pons and medulla increased in parallel with the size of
the saccade according to the SC motor map. The results suggest that there are multiple
projections directly onto OPNs from the rostral SC but not from the caudal SC associated with large
gaze shifts. The efferents from the rostral pole of the motor map may subserve the suppression of
saccades during visual ﬁxation, and those from the small horizontal saccade zone could inhibit
anatagonist premotor circuits. J. Comp. Neurol. 413:55–67, 1999.
r 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Indexing terms: saccade generator; predorsal bundle; rostral pole of the superior colliculus motor
map; nucleus raphe interpositus; superior colliculus ipsilateral descending
pathway; gaze ﬁxation
The neural network of omnipause neurons (OPNs), excita-
tory burst neurons (EBNs), and inhibitory burst neurons
(IBNs) form the basic circuitry of the saccade generator.
The OPNs in the raphe of the caudal pons are character-
ized by a high, tonic ﬁring-rate that stops abruptly before
every type of rapid eye movement, or saccade. A reciprocal
discharge pattern is found in neurons lying more lateral in
the adjacent paramedian pontine reticular formation
(PPRF): their burst of activity before each horizontal
saccade provides the saccade-related input to abducens
motoneurons (Henn and Cohen, 1976; Keller, 1974; Lus-
chei and Fuchs, 1972). The excitatory burst neurons
Grant sponsor: German Research Council; Grant number: SFB 462/B3.
*Correspondence to: Prof. J.A. Bu¨ttner-Ennever, Institute of Anatomy,
Ludwig-Maximilian University, Pettenkoferstrasse 11, D-80336 Munich,
Germany. E-mail: buettner@anat.med.uni-muenchen.de
Received 20 April 1998; Revised 14 June 1999; Accepted 29 June 1999
THE JOURNAL OF COMPARATIVE NEUROLOGY 413:55–67 (1999)
r 1999 WILEY-LISS, INC.
 (EBNs) in the PPRF were shown to project directly to
ipsilateral abducens motoneurons (Bu¨ttner-Ennever and
Henn, 1976; Strassman et al., 1986a), and inhibitory burst
neurons (IBNs) projected to the contralateral abducens
motoneurons (Strassman et al., 1986b; for reviews, see
Fuchs et al., 1985; Bu¨ttner-Ennever and Bu¨ttner, 1988;
Moschovakis and Highstein, 1994). The early studies in
behaving monkeys suggested that the OPNs could provide
an ‘inhibitory gate’ for the generation of a saccade if their
discharge tonically suppressed the activity of premotor
burst neurons in the intersaccadic interval. The pause in
OPN discharge just before a saccade would release the
EBN and IBN premotor burst neurons from inhibition,
then allow them to directly excite the extraocular motoneu-
rons, and generate a saccade. This hypothesis was substan-
tiated by several subsequent studies: electrical stimula-
tion of the OPN area stopped saccades in midﬂight (Keller,
1977; King and Fuchs, 1977); reconstruction of the axonal
branches of identiﬁed, intracellularly-ﬁlled OPNs showed
that they terminated over the EBN and IBN pools (Ohgaki
et al., 1987; Strassman et al., 1987); and anatomic studies
demonstrated that OPNs used glycine as their inhibitory
transmitter (Horn et al., 1994).
In monkeys, the OPNs are located in a cytoarchitectur-
ally distinct raphe cell group, the nucleus raphe interposi-
tus (RIP), which lies between the nucleus raphe pontis and
the nucleus raphe magnus (Bu¨ttner-Ennever et al., 1988;
Langer and Kaneko, 1990). The cells of the RIP form a thin
row of neurons aligned on either side of the midline; they
are medium-sized and relatively homogeneous, and they
have prominent dendrites oriented horizontally that ex-
tend across the midline. In all species that have been
studied so far, the rootlets of the abducens nerve provide a
reliable landmark for the location of OPNs: the RIP cells
lie on either side of the midline at the level of the abducens
rootlets in the tegmentum (Horn et al., 1994). Langer and
Kaneko (1990) estimated that 85% of the neurons in the
RIP were OPNs.
The superior colliculus (SC) is essential for generating
coordinated orienting responses of the eye and head to
objects of interest (for review, see Sparks and Hartwich-
Young, 1989). In primates, the orienting response is com-
prised of a saccade, which is accompanied by a head
movement for large gaze shifts. The generation of the
orienting head movement is known to be dependent on the
tectoreticular-reticulospinal connections rather than a di-
rect tectospinal pathway (Isa et al., 1992; Grantyn et al.,
1987, 1993; Olivier et al., 1993): The pontine and medul-
lary reticular formation are particularly important in the
collicular control of horizontal head movements (Harting,
1977; Isa et al., 1992; May and Porter, 1992; Cowie and
Robinson, 1994; Cowie et al., 1994; Robinson et al., 1994).
The connectivity between the SC and the saccade genera-
tor has been considered in several reviews (Sparks and
Mays, 1990; Olivier et al., 1993; Moschovakis and High-
stein, 1994; Moschovakis, 1996; Moschovakis et al., 1996).
All models agree that the SC must project to a group of
inhibitory long-lead burst neurons (LBNs), the location of
which remains unknown, that silence the OPNs before a
saccade and allow the SC, perhaps through the LBNs, to
drive the disinhibited EBNs and produce a saccade (Ray-
bourn and Keller, 1977; Miyashita and Hikosaka, 1996;
Scudder et al., 1996b). Other studies of SC projections to
the saccade generator have emphasized a direct input from
the SC (Olivier et al., 1993; Chimoto et al., 1996a,b;
Grantyn et al., 1996).
Direct stimulation of the SC intermediate layers evokes
saccades, the size and direction of which depend on the
location of the stimulating electrode. According to the SC
motor map of Robinson (1972), large horizontal saccades
are represented caudally, and small horizontal saccades
are represented rostrally. The medial regions of the SC are
associated with an upward saccadic component, and the
lateral regions of the SC are associated with a downward
saccadic component. The rostral pole of the motor map in
the SC is the region where the fovea is represented, and, in
the monkey, it lies lateral and < 1 mm caudal to the most
anterior tip of the SC (see Fig. 1).
Recently, OPNs have received increasing attention due
to their similarity in ﬁring pattern to a subset of neurons
called ﬁxation cells or tectal pause neurons that are found
only in the rostral pole of the SC (cat: Munoz and Guitton,
1989, 1991; Peck, 1989; monkey: Munoz and Wurtz, 1993a;
Everling et al., 1998). The tectal pause neurons ﬁre with a
high activity when the animal ﬁxates a visual target, then,
shortly before a saccade, they pause, like OPNs. Stimula-
tion of the SC rostral pole suppresses saccades and head
motion (Munoz and Wurtz, 1993b; Pare´ and Guitton,
1994), and the local application of muscimol (which mimics
the action of the inhibitory transmitter g-aminobutyric
acid) to the rostral SC results in the production of intru-
sion saccades (Munoz and Wurtz, 1993b). On the basis of
these studies, it was proposed that the rostral pole of the
SC has an excitatory projection to the OPNs that would
serve to suppress saccade generation and maintain visual
ﬁxation (Munoz and Guitton, 1989, 1991; Munoz and
Wurtz, 1993a,b; Pare´ and Guitton, 1994).
Electrophysiological studies ﬁrst demonstrated the exis-
tence of a projection from the SC to OPNs (Raybourn and
Keller, 1977), and, when OPNs were identiﬁed anatomi-
cally in the RIP, tract-tracing experiments conﬁrmed this
Abbreviations

BSC

DTN

EBN

INF

MLF

NRM

NRPC

NRTP

NVI

NVII

OLN

PAG

PDB

PGD

PH
nucleus prepositus hypoglossi
PPT

PUL

RIP

RM

SG

SGI

SO

56
J.A. BU¨ TTNER-ENNEVER ET AL.

Projections From the Superior Colliculus
Motor Map to Omnipause Neurons
JEAN A. BU¨ TTNER-ENNEVER,1* ANJA K.E. HORN,1 V. HENN,2 AND B. COHEN3
1Institute of Anatomy, Ludwig-Maximilian University, D-80336 Munich, Germany
2Department of Neurology, University Hospital Zurich, CH-8091 Zurich, Switzerland
3Department of Neurology, Mount Sinai Medical School, City University of New York,
Descending projections from the superior colliculus (SC) motor map to the saccadic
omnipause neurons (OPNs) were examined in monkeys by using anterograde transport of
tritiated leucine. The SC was divided into three zones: the rostral pole of the motor map, a
small horizontal saccade zone in central SC, and a large horizontal saccade zone in caudal SC.
Tracer injections into the intermediate layers of the three zones led to different patterns of
silver grain deposits in and around nucleus raphe interpositus (RIP), which contains the
OPNs: 1) From the rostral pole of the motor map, coarse axon branches of the crossed
predorsal bundle spread medially into the RIP, branched, and terminated predominantly
unilaterally over cells on the same side. 2) From the small horizontal saccade zone, the axon
branches were of a ﬁner caliber and terminated diffusely in the RIP, mainly on the same side.
3) From the large horizontal saccade zone, no terminal labeling was found within the RIP. 4)
From the rostral pole of the motor map and small horizontal saccade zone, ﬁber branches from
the ipsilateral descending pathway terminated diffusely over RIP. 5) In addition, terminal
labeling in reticulospinal areas of the pons and medulla increased in parallel with the size of
the saccade according to the SC motor map. The results suggest that there are multiple
projections directly onto OPNs from the rostral SC but not from the caudal SC associated with large
gaze shifts. The efferents from the rostral pole of the motor map may subserve the suppression of
saccades during visual ﬁxation, and those from the small horizontal saccade zone could inhibit
anatagonist premotor circuits. J. Comp. Neurol. 413:55–67, 1999.

result (Bu¨ttner-Ennever et al., 1988). The projection arises
predominantly from the rostral SC (Bu¨ttner-Ennever and
Horn, 1994; Pare´ and Guitton, 1994; Chimoto et al., 1996b;
Sato and Ohtsuka, 1996). More recent investigations have
shown that other regions of the SC also may project to
OPNs: Terminal arborizations in the OPN area arise from
tectoreticular neurons encoding eye movements (cat: Ol-
ivier et al., 1993; monkey: Scudder et al., 1996a). Gandhi
and Keller (1997) antidromically activated many SC neu-
rons antidromically outside the rostral pole from the OPN
Until now, there has been no systematic study of the
anatomical projections from different regions of the SC
motor map to the OPN area. In this study, we have used
neuroanatomical tract-tracing methods to compare the
projections from the rostral, central, and caudal SC to the
RIP, where the OPNs lie, and we present evidence here for
three different types of afferents. The experiments were
carried out in monkey, because the primate RIP nucleus is
deﬁned most clearly. Initial results have been reported
elsewhere (Bu¨ttner-Ennever and Horn, 1994; Bu¨ttner-
Anterograde transport of tritiated leucine
Macaque monkeys received an injection of tritiated
leucine ([3H]leucine) into the region of the SC. Under
general anesthesia and aseptic conditions, a small hole
was trephined in the skull according to the coordinates of
the atlas (Snider and Lee, 1961; Shanta et al., 1968).
[3H]leucine (0.2–1.0 µl) in distilled water (100 µCI/µl) was
injected by pressure through a glass micropipette. Six
cases were selected in which the injection ﬁlled intermedi-
ate layers of the SC and resulted in tracer deposits in the
caudal midline pons. One additional case, C66, was se-
lected as a control injection into the pretectum that did not
In three experiments (cases C61, B11, and C66), the
coordinates of the injection site according to the SC motor
map were obtained by inserting a stimulating electrode
and evoking the appropriate eye movements. The place-
ment was adjusted until the stimulation evoked saccades
of zero amplitude (C61), large saccades (B11), or horizontal
nystagmus (C66; control): the stimulation electrode was
then withdrawn, and the micropipette was introduced as
near as possible to the same location. In three other
experiments (cases A11, A3, and B44), the injection site
was localized after deﬁning the stereotaxic coordinates of
the oculomotor/trochlear nucleus border with microstimu-
lation (3–10 µA). An insulated tungsten electrode with
an < 50-µm exposed tip length was used, and tracks were
searched with microstimulation until appropriate eye
movements for the oculomotor/trochlear nuclei border
were observed. From these coordinates, the injection site
was calculated according to the atlas of Shanta et al.
(1968). In case Z25, the injection was placed according to
stereotaxic coordinates alone (Shantha et al., 1968).
After a survival time of 2–3 weeks, the animals were
killed with an overdose of Nembutal (80 mg/kg body
weight) and transcardially perfused with 0.9% saline
(35°C) followed by 2% paraformaldehyde with 1% glutaral-
dehyde in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution, pH 7.4. The
brains were immersed in 10% sucrose in 0.1 M phosphate
buffer, pH7.4, and transferred to 30% sucrose for 4 days.
The brain was cut at 40 µm on a freezing-microtome in the
transverse plane. In two cases, the caudal blocks were
embedded in paraffin and cut at 15 µm in the sagittal
plane. The sections were mounted on gelatinized slides,
dewaxed, rehydrated, and dried in the oven for 48 hours at
40°C. In the darkroom, the slides were dipped in Kodak
NTB-3 or NTB-2 nuclear track emulsion (Eastman-Kodak,
Rochester, NY) diluted 1:1 with distilled water and dried
for 4 hours. After exposures of 4 weeks or 8 weeks at 4°C,
depending on the emulsion used, the slides were developed
in Kodak D-19 developer for 4 minutes at 12–15°C and
ﬁxed in Tetanal superﬁx (diluted 1:9 in distilled water) for
10 minutes. After washing for 2 hours in running water,
the sections were counterstained with cresyl violet, dehy-
drated, and coverslipped with Depex. The sections were
examined and photographed by using a light microscope
under darkﬁeld and brightﬁeld illumination.
All injection sites are depicted on the left side to
facilitate the analysis. Because the SC efferents to the
midline pons are known to originate from the SC interme-
diate layers (May and Porter, 1992), the uptake area of this
layer only is plotted in Figure 1. The locations of the
injection sites on the SC motor map in Figure 1 were
reconstructed from the estimated tracer uptake-areas in
the SC intermediate layers on consecutive sections through
the injection site. The plots of each uptake area were
transferred onto the motor map of Robinson (1972) by
normalizing the dimensions of the SC. Despite the inher-
ent inaccuracies of this method, the error would not affect
the gross subdivision of the injection sites into the three
categories: rostral pole, medial colliculus, and caudal
Images of brightﬁeld photomicrographs (Figs. 2, 6) were
digitalized by using a 3-CCD video camera mounted on a
The location of the tritiated leucine ([3H]leucine) injection
sites within the superior colliculus (SC). The estimated uptake areas
within the intermediate layer (layer IV) are plotted onto a normalized
dorsal view of the left SC. Topographic motor map based on Robinson
(1972) has been superimposed. For abbreviations, see list.
SUPERIOR COLLICULUS PROJECTIONS TO OPNS
Digitally processed photomicrographs of the superior collicu-
lus (SC) injections sites centered on the injection tract and involving
the rostral pole (A), the small horizontal saccade zone (C), and the
large horizontal saccade zone (E). A: Tracer ﬁlls the stratum opticum
(SO) and the upper stratum griseum intermediale (SGI) in the
dorsolateral SC, where the rostral pole of the motor map is repre-
sented, with involvement of the pretectum and pulvinar. B: Tracer
deposits lie in the most anterior SC, in intermediate and deep layers,
and in the pretectum and the nucleus of the posterior commissure.
C: Tracer deposit in the small horizontal saccade zone of the SGI
medial and slightly caudal to the site shown in A. D: Involvement of
the SO and the upper SGI in uptake area. E: Small deposit in the
upper SGI of the large horizontal saccade zone in the caudal SC.
F: Large tracer deposit in the lateral SC ﬁlling the intermediate layers
of the caudal and rostral SC. For abbreviations, see list. Scale bar 5
microscope. The images were captured with 4.0 Adobe
Photoshop software (Adobe Systems, Mountain View, CA).
After conversion into black and white, the sharpness,
contrast, and brightness were adjusted to reﬂect the
appearance of the labeling seen through the microscope.
All experimental procedures conformed to the state and
university regulations on laboratory animal care, includ-
ing the National Institutes of Health revised guidelines,
and were approved by their animal care officers and
institutional animal care and use committees.
The six cases with [3H]leucine deposits in the SC were
separated into three categories according to the location of
the injection site with respect to the motor map: 1) the
rostral pole group, in which the fovea is represented (case
C61); 2) the small horizontal saccade group (cases B11,
A11, and Z25), which injections were located roughly
within 2–10° meridians; and 3) the large horizontal sac-
cade group (case A3), in which injections were located
within 10–40° meridians. The sixth case (B44) involved all
three zones and extended into the SC deep layers. The
extent of the tracer uptake area within the intermediate
layers of SC is plotted on the motor map of Robinson’s
(1972; Fig. 1). The actual injection sites are shown in
Figure 2. Case C66 served as a control, because the uptake
area lay within the nucleus of the optic tract (NOT) dorsal
to the rostral SC. It spread into the pulvinar but did not
invade the colliculus: no crossed predorsal bundle (PDB)
was labeled, and no tracer deposits were found in the
medial pontine or medullary reticular formation.
The crossed descending PDB was labeled strongly from
all collicular injections. In the caudal pons, it approached
the midline between the rootlets of the abducens nerve
(NVI) and passed laterally to the RIP (i.e., the OPN area).
Different patterns of tracer deposits were found in the RIP
according to the location of the SC injection on the motor
map. Figure 3 shows the location of the RIP in detail and a
typical photomicrograph demonstrating the regular ar-
rangement of cells on either side of the midline. The tracer
deposits in the RIP and the surrounding area are drawn in
Figure 4 (arrows), which was photographed under dark-
ﬁeld illumination in Figure 5 and at high-power magniﬁca-
tion, brightﬁeld illumination in Figure 6.
Horizontal axonal branches of the predorsal bundle
turned medially into the RIP, branched, and gave rise to
clusters of silver grains mainly around the cell bodies on
the same side (Figs. 4A, 5A,B, 6A,B). Some of the axonal
branches entering the RIP had large diameters (3–5 µm

SUPERIOR COLLICULUS PROJECTIONS TO OPNS

branches and cells on the right side of the raphe interpositus (RIP) after
tracer injection into left superior colliculus (SC). Open arrows indicate the
location of the midline. Deposits in the rostral pole (A,B) label some coarse
axon branches (solid arrow), which terminate in clusters. The star in A
marks the cell indicated by an open arrow in Figure 5B. In C and D, the
labeling from the small saccade zone is more diffuse and arises from ﬁner
axonal branches (solid arrow). From the large saccade zone (E,F), ﬁbers of
the predorsal bundle on the right send branches laterally (solid arrow) but
send very few to the RIP: no ﬁne terminal labeling is evident over the cells
(marked with a star in E and F). In G and F, axon branches from the
uncrossed lateral descending pathway approach the RIP from the left side
(solid arrows) and terminate in the RIP bilaterally. The scale bar for each
pair of ﬁgures (AB, CD, EF, and GH) represented 20 µm, except in G where

branches were evident, and terminal labeling was more
homogeneous and less clustered than that from the rostral
pole of the motor map (compare Fig. 6C,D with Fig. 6A,B).
Some uncrossed, laterally descending ﬁbers turned medi-
ally to supply the RIP (Fig. 4B).
Labeled branches from the PDB also spread laterally
into the NRPC and farther caudally into the nucleus
reticularis gigantocellularis. In addition, the INF, a group
of large chromatic cells within the fascicles of the medial
longitudinal fasciculus, received terminal deposits (see
The second injection involving the small horizontal
saccade zone (case Z25) extended only into the upper parts
of the intermediate layer of the SC (Figs. 1, 2D). The RIP
was supplied by a few single, thin-caliber ﬁbers from both
the contralateral PDB and the ipsilateral descending
pathway. Some of the latter ﬁbers terminated ventral to
RIP in the nucleus raphe magnus. Labeled deposits also
were found in the PGD but were found nowhere else in the
medullary reticular formation.
The third small horizontal saccade zone injection (case
A11) included an area on the motor map with a large
upward component near the border with the pretectum
(Figs. 1, 2B). It did not include the rostral pole. The
character of labeling in that RIP was similar to that seen
in case B11, with thin ﬁber branches terminating diffusely
throughout the neuropil, mainly unilaterally. Many ipsilat-
eral ﬁbers from the uncrossed lateral pathway ran medi-
ally and terminated in the RIP (Fig. 6H). Another group of
ipsilateral ﬁbers terminated ventral to the RIP in the
nucleus raphe magnus. Similar to case B11, the reticular
formation was labeled but far more heavily. In addition,
small deposits lay within the INF and the facial nucleus
Large horizontal saccade zone (case A3)
The uptake area in caseA3 lay close to the 20° horizontal
meridian in an area of the motor map with a signiﬁcant
downward component (Figs. 1, 2E). It included the interme-
diate layers and also extended into the deep layers. No
terminal branching was seen in the RIP. The crossed PDB
was labeled heavily, but branches from it at the level of the
RIP extended away from the midline into the NRPC (Figs.
5E and 6E solid arrow). The cells and neuropil of the RIP
were free of terminal labeling (Figs.5E,F, 6E,F, stars). The
uncrossed lateral descending pathway was labeled strongly,
but very few ﬁbers continued to the level of the abducens
rootlets and none continued into the RIP. The pontine and
medullary reticular formation were labeled heavily. Silver
grain deposits were found in the INF, in part of the nucleus
supragenualis, and in the facial nucleus bilaterally.
Large injection involving all three zones
The injection in case B44 ﬁlled both the intermediate
and deep layers of the SC in the two saccade zones and
extended into the rim of the rostral pole (Figs. 1, 2F). The
neuropil of the RIP was labeled diffusely on the side
adjacent to the PDB, as in case B11. In addition, a few cell
bodies on either side of the RIP received labeled collaterals
and terminals from coarser axonal branches (3–5 µ) through
the crossed PDB. Individual ﬁbers of comparable diameter
from the ipsilateral descending pathway also terminated
in the RIP. Parts of the RIP were free of transported tracer.
Immediately lateral to the RIP, the reticular formation
was labeled strongly with ﬁne deposits, indicating axon
terminals. Cell groups within the medial longitudinal
fasciculus (INF) and the paramedian reticular nucleus of
the medulla were labeled strongly.
In summary, we found that, from the rostral pole of the
SC motor map, coarse axonal branches terminated within
the RIP. The deposits clustered around cells on the same
side of the RIP as the descending PDB, leaving the row of
neurons on the other side relatively free of silver grain
deposits. From the small horizontal saccade zone, the
character of the SC afferents within the RIP was different:
the axonal branches generally were thinner and branched
diffusely throughout the RIP, again predominantly on the
one side. The large horizontal saccade zone gave rise to no
terminal labeling within the RIP. An additional source of
afferents to the RIP arose from the uncrossed lateral
descending pathways of the rostral and central SC and
supplied both sides of the RIP. These results are summa-
rized by the diagrams in Figure 7.
We describe for the ﬁrst time three different patterns of
collicular inputs directly into the omnipause area, in the
RIP. In general, the SC is expected to project not to the
OPNs but to the premotor gaze circuits, including LBNs,
which inhibit OPNs and thereby produce a gaze shift (for
reviews, see Sparks and Mays, 1990; Moschovakis and
Highstein, 1994; Moschovakis et al., 1996). A direct excita-
tory projection to OPNs would inhibit saccade generation
and would not be appropriate during a change of gaze. The
SC rostral pole is an exception to this, because it is thought
to participate in gaze ﬁxation, and a projection to OPNs
would contribute to the stabilization of the eyes. However,
we ﬁnd that the direct projections to OPNs are not
conﬁned to the rostral pole, a result that is supported by
several other studies (Scudder et al., 1996a; Gandhi and
Keller, 1997; Everling et al., 1998).
Rostral pole and small horizontal saccade
zone have different terminal patterns
In our initial report on the SC-OPN projection (Bu¨ttner-
Ennever et al., 1988), we established the existence of the
RIP for the ﬁrst time and used the fact that it was targeted
by SC efferents as a proof that the RIP contained OPNs.
We did not analyze the types of terminal patterns in the
RIP or relate the location of the injection sites to the motor
map of Robinson (1972). Two of these cases have been
reanalyzed in detail for this study (cases C61 and B11).
The injection in case B11 was referred to previously as
being located in the ‘caudal SC,’ whereas, relative to the
motor map, the injection is located in the small horizontal
saccade zone (central SC) and projects to the RIP (see
showed that efferent PDB ﬁbers from the rostral pole of the
motor map gave rise to a pattern of terminals arising from
thick axon collaterals that are not characteristic of injec-
tions into the rest of the SC. Therefore, they appear to
originate from a relatively restricted region of the interme-
diate SC layer (Fig. 2A, circle). The region is immediately
adjacent to the dorsal terminal nucleus. However, the
origin of a pathway can never be localized exactly by using
Projections from the SC to the RIP have been described
anatomically in several studies on cat and monkey
SUPERIOR COLLICULUS PROJECTIONS TO OPNS
(Bu¨ttner-Ennever and Horn, 1994; Sato and Ohtsuka,
1996; Scudder et al., 1996a). Here for the ﬁrst time we
describe a distinctive projection pattern only from the
rostral pole region that we believe may originate from the
tectal pause neurons or from the ﬁxation neurons in
trained monkeys and cats (monkey: Munoz and Wurtz,
1993a,b; Everling et al., 1998; cat: Munoz and Guitton,
1989, 1991; Peck, 1989; Peck and Baro, 1997). The ﬁxation
cells have been located more accurately in the lower parts
of the intermediate sublamina, ventral to SC burst cells,
and are thought to form part of the rostral continuation of
the ‘build-up’ neuronal layer (Munoz and Wurtz, 1993a,
1995; Gandhi and Keller, 1997). This hypothesis is repre-
sented in the diagrams in Figure 7A. Another reason to
believe that the projection from the rostral pole originates
from tectal pause neurons is that only in the rostral pole
case (C61) were coarse ﬁbers similar to those entering the
RIP detected in the ipsilateral nucleus reticularis teg-
menti pontis (Fig. 4A, section 45, star), where a type of
ﬁxation neuron has been reported (Crandall and Keller,
1985). These results will be considered in a separate
Diagrams of four different types of superior colliculus (SC)
inputs to the omnipause neuron (OPN) region. Stratum griseum
intermediale (SGI) represents the intermediate layers of the SC.
A: The OPN input from the SGI rostral pole is assumed to arise from
large cells (X-cell type). They target mainly contralateral OPNs, and
gaze-ﬁxation would result from simultaneous activation of the rostral
poles of both sides. B: The small saccade zone input to OPNs is of a
different character: It originates at least in part from small, long-lead
burst neurons (T-cells) and could inhibit the contralateral premotor
network before an ipsilateral saccade. Additional input from the larger
cells cannot be excluded. C: The large saccade zone does not terminate
in the OPN area. D: Fibers from the SC uncrossed lateral descending
pathway terminate in the OPN area bilaterally. The box labeled
‘premotor gaze circuits’ represents cell groups that generate gaze
shifts, such as reticulospinal neurons, excitatory burst neurons (EBNs),
inhibitory burst neurons, and ‘saccadic-trigger cells,’ which inhibit
OPNs. Monosynaptic inputs to OPNs from the SC are assumed to be
Small horizontal saccade zone.
OPN area from the small saccade zone was characterized
by the arborization of thin axonal branches (Fig. 6C,D).
Collicular efferents in the crossed PDB can be classiﬁed
morphologically into two main populations: the small
T-cells, which lie in the stratum opticum and the interme-
diate layers, and the larger X-cells, which are scattered
throughout the intermediate layers and occasionally in the
stratum opticum (Moschovakis and Karabelas, 1985; Mos-
chovakis et al., 1988a,b). In the primate SC, LBNs were of
the T-cell class; but the X-cells were not active during the
spontaneous saccades paradigm used in this study (Moscho-
vakis et al., 1988b). However, in cat, SC neurons that
encode primarily the intended head movement but also are
active for eye movements have been identiﬁed as X-cells
(Olivier et al., 1993). To date, the correlation between
X-cells and the physiological classiﬁcation of build-up
neurons in behaving monkey has not been established.
The projection from the small horizontal saccade zone to
the RIP seen in this study theoretically may arise from
either the T-cell or the X-cell population, based on the
evidence of intracellular reconstructions. In primates, an
LBN (T-cell) encoding small saccades was shown to provide
boutons diffusely over the RIP (Scudder et al., 1996a),
whereas, in one of four X-cells, terminals were demon-
strated in the OPN area in cat (Olivier et al., 1993).
Because of the species differences between cat and monkey
in tectoreticular cell populations, we conclude that the
diffuse projection to the OPN area from the small horizon-
tal saccade zone reﬂects, at least in part, an LBN (T-cell)
Large horizontal saccade zone does not
The intermediate layers of the caudal colliculus do not
provide afferents to the RIP nucleus (Fig. 7C) but project
strongly to reticulospinal regions of the pons and medulla.
In this study, the intensity of SC projections to all of these
regions was seen to increase with gaze displacement
according to the size of the horizontal or the vertical (case
A11) saccade component on the SC motor map. Our results
can be interpreted as supporting the hypothesis based on
data from the cat that the colliculus projection contributes
to the metrics of gaze saccades (Grantyn et al., 1993;
Olivier et al., 1993; Pare´ et al., 1994; Chimoto et al.,
With regard to the lack of input to the RIP from the
caudal SC, the current experiments showed that, in gen-
eral, the caudal SC does not send terminal axon branches
into the RIP area. This result is supported by Gandhi and
Keller (1997), who found a strong decrease from the rostral
SC to the caudal SC in the number of build-up neurons
that could be excited antidromically from the OPN area. In
contrast, Langer and Kaneko (1990) found a high density
of retrogradely ﬁlled cells in the caudal SC after injections
into the OPN area. It is extremely difficult to demonstrate
unequivocally inputs to the OPNs in the RIP by using
retrograde neuronal tracers, because all retrograde trac-
ers are taken up by damaged ﬁbers of passage. The PDB
veers so close to the RIP along its whole length (50–100
µm; see Fig. 6E,F) that even the luckiest injection into the
OPNs may be expected to damage the PDB (or at least
some crossing ﬁbers) and can lead to an overestimation of
inputs to the OPN area. A similar argument applies to
some of the SC neurons ﬁred antidromically from stimula-
tion of the OPN area (Gandhi and Keller, 1997).
Conversely, one drawback of the current study was due
to the laminar character of SC. The PDB, through which
the SC controls gaze circuits, originates mainly from
neurons in the upper half of the intermediate layers,
whereas the lower intermediate layer projects to the
lateral reticular formation and the spinal cord (Moscho-
vakis et al., 1988a,b; May and Porter, 1992). The injection
in case A3 into the large horizontal saccade zone was
small, and, although it covered the lower intermediate
layer well, it may have ﬁlled the upper intermediate layers
of the SC only partially (Fig. 2E) and, thus, may have
missed a small projection from the caudal SC. Our large
injection into all layers of the caudal SC (case B44; see Fig.
2F) did not resolve the problem, because it also involved
the upper intermediate layers of the small horizontal
saccade zone that already project to the OPN area. How-
ever, it is not clear that this could resolve the discrepancy
with the results of Langer and Kaneko (1990).
SC projects unilaterally to the OPN area
Some SC LBNs show a burst of activity before a saccade
and project to EBN regions (Scudder et al., 1996a). At ﬁrst
glance, it is hard to see why they should project simulta-
neously to the OPNs, which inhibit the burst neurons and
oppose eye movement. However, one fact about OPNs that
is not generally recognized is that the majority of OPNs
send their axons across the midline to innervate the EBN
and IBN burst populations on the contralateral side.
Ohgaki et al. (1987) found that 13 of a group of 16 OPNs
projected contralaterally in the cat. It is not clear what
percentage of OPNs crosses the midline in the monkey
(Strassman et al., 1987). If the LBNs of the SC in the small
horizontal saccade zone project to the contralateral OPNs,
which, themselves, have crossing axons, then the saccade-
related activation of the OPNs will lead to an inhibition of
the antagonistic burst population on the same side as the
active SC. This is illustrated by the diagram in Figure 7B.
The RIP is comprised of two rows of OPNs. The unilater-
ality of the SC input from both the rostral pole and the
small horizontal saccade zone to the OPNs was not ex-
pected, and it raised questions regarding the extent to
which and the manner in which OPN pools on each side are
coordinated. In the case of visual ﬁxation, both SC rostral
poles would be active simultaneously; thus, the activation
of OPNs (with crossed axons) would suppress the activity
of the burst neuron pools of each side in a coordinated
Identiﬁcation of different populations by
[3H]leucine was used as an anterograde tracer in this
study. It has the advantage of being taken up by cell bodies
only and not by ﬁbers of passage. The efferents of all cell
groups within the uptake area are labeled, and the method
demonstrates the projections of cell populations that may
be missed otherwise in studies that select neurons on the
basis of one speciﬁc physiological property. [3H]leucine is
less well suited to follow labeled terminal branches. First,
the labeling reﬂects only the tracer deposits in the upper 2
µm of the section and thus does not correlate with the Nissl
picture that is generated mainly by the rest of the 40-µm-
thick section. However, the detection of axon terminals is
reliable and depends on demonstrating a ﬁne and irregu-
SUPERIOR COLLICULUS PROJECTIONS TO OPNS
lar pattern of silver grains. The pattern of silver grains
was more clustered from rostral pole afferents than from
other regions of the SC. A second disadvantage is that the
resolution of the autoradiographic technique, assuming a
point source of tritium, depends on the distance of the
labeled element from the emulsion (, 40 µm), the penetra-
tion of the b-rays through the emulsion (1–2 µm), and the
size of the silver grains of the nuclear track emulsion
(NTB2, 0.26 µm; NTB3, 0.34 µm; Ku¨nzle, 1989). By using
our methods, this results in a resolution of between 1 µm
and 2 µm; this scatter still enabled us to distinguish
between thinner (1–3 µm; see Fig. 6D,G) and thicker (3–5
µm) axonal branches in the RIP (Fig. 6A,B).
We can identify two different patterns of projections to
OPN area (compare Fig. 6A,B with Fig. 6C,D). However,
can the thickness of the axonal branches entering the RIP
from the PDB be used as evidence to support our hypoth-
esis that the thick ﬁbers originate from larger cells in the
lower intermediate layers? Some caution with that assump-
tion is necessary, because a change in axon diameter with
distance from the cell body has been well documented in
tectal efferents (Scudder et al., 1996a). On the other hand,
for tectal neurons, there is a high correlation between cell
size and axon size; that is, large axons arise from large
cells, and small axons arise from small cells (Moschovakis
et al., 1988b). Furthermore, a Golgi study of neuronal
proﬁles in the inferior colliculus led to the conclusion that
‘‘. . .the coarsest ﬁbers maintain relatively uniform diam-
eter and have the thickest side branches and largest
endings often arranged in relatively compact clusters. The
thin axons have skinny side branches. . .’’ (Oliver and
Morest, 1984). This indirectly supports our supposition.
The coarse nature of the axonal branches from the rostral
pole seen in the RIP, therefore, is suggestive of their origin
from the larger X-cell population rather than the smaller
T-cell population. Munoz et al. (1995a) tentatively as-
sumed that their ‘tectal pause neurons’(ﬁxation cells) were
part of the X-cell population (i.e., large neurons) on the
basis of their depth in the intermediate layers. Conversely,
the ﬁne nature of terminals in the RIP from the small
horizontal saccade zone efferents suggests that they origi-
nate from the smaller T-cells (i.e., LBNs; Scudder et al.,
1996a). A contribution of afferents from a class of larger,
head movement-related, X-cells to the OPN, as in the cat
(Olivier et al., 1993), cannot be ruled out in the monkey.
However, it appears to be less likely in light of a recent
report by Phillips et al. (1999), in which the OPN ﬁring
patterns in the head-free behaving monkey correlated
with only that portion of gaze movement that is com-
manded by the saccade generator. This hypothesis is
depicted by the diagram in Figure 7A,B.
Ipsilateral descending SC-OPN pathway
We found that a group of uncrossed tectofugal ﬁbers
descending laterally also projected to the OPN area (Fig.
7D). Their function is unknown. The ipsilateral pathway
has been associated with the visual avoidance responses
(Dean et al., 1988; Cowie and Holstege, 1992). Studies on
the primate show that a class of neurons (L-neurons)
located mainly in superﬁcial layers and carrying visual
information have axons that contribute to this pathway
(Moschovakis et al., 1988a). It is possible that OPNs
receive the visual information reported by Evinger et al.
(1981) through this uncrossed path.
Direct afferents to the OPNs from the SC rostral pole
were predicted over 10 years ago (Munoz and Guitton,
1989). Now, there is mounting evidence for the existence of
additional direct inputs from the colliculus to OPNs,
subserving small saccades (Scudder et al., 1996a), vision
(Evinger et al., 1981), and accommodation (Ohtsuka and
Nagasaka, 1999) or arising from build-up neurons (Gandhi
and Keller, 1997; Everling et al., 1998). The neuroanatomy
of collicular efferents to the OPNs also reﬂects this diver-
